title: Replication of Angrist & Lavy (1999)
text:
Does shrinking class size actually help kids learn? The simple correlation in observational data is famously misleading: small classes tend to show up in wealthy, high-performing schools, while large classes pile up in disadvantaged schools that also happen to be stretched thin along every other dimension. Regressing test scores on class size therefore mostly picks up the socioeconomic context of the student, not the pedagogical effect of the classroom.
Angrist and Lavy (1999) solve this problem with a quintessentially Israeli natural experiment. Since the twelfth century, the educational guidance of Rabbi Moses ben Maimon (“Maimonides”) has held that a teacher may instruct up to forty students. When a cohort exceeds forty, a second class must be opened; when it exceeds eighty, a third; and so on. The Israeli Ministry of Education adopted this rule as official policy. As a result, class size is a deterministic, sawtooth function of grade enrollment: a school with 40 fourth-graders teaches them in one class of 40, but a school with 41 fourth-graders splits them into two classes that average 20.5. Schools sitting on either side of 40 (or 80, or 120) are otherwise similar, but their children are assigned to dramatically different class sizes by a rule nobody designed for research purposes. That discontinuity provides variation in class size that is plausibly unrelated to the usual confounders, which the authors exploit with a regression discontinuity design (RDD) — using Maimonides’ rule as an instrument for (or a reduced-form predictor of) actual class size.
In this post I focus on the 4th-grade sample. The Main Analysis section below replicates everything empirical the assignment asks for:

Table II, columns 7 and 10 — the naive OLS of reading and math scores on class size.
Table III Panel A, columns 7, 9, and 11 — the reduced-form RDD estimates using the Maimonides prediction \(f_{sc}\) in place of actual class size.
Figure 1, Panel B — the graphical heart of the paper, plotting actual class size against the Maimonides prediction as a function of enrollment.

The contrast between Table II and Table III is the whole point: the naive OLS reports that bigger classes go with better scores, while the RDD flips the sign and shows exactly what Maimonides would have predicted — fewer kids per teacher helps. The Something Additional section at the end steps back from the numbers to explain why a regression discontinuity design can recover a causal effect from observational data, using this paper as the running example.

title: Replication of Angrist & Lavy (1999)
text:
Does shrinking class size actually help kids learn? The simple correlation in observational data is famously misleading: small classes tend to show up in wealthy, high-performing schools, while large classes pile up in disadvantaged schools that also happen to be stretched thin along every other dimension. Angrist and Lavy (1999) solve this problem with a quintessentially Israeli natural experiment. Table III Panel A, columns 7, 9, and 11 — the reduced-form RDD estimates using the Maimonides prediction \(f_{sc}\) in place of actual class size.

title: Replication of Angrist & Lavy (1999)
text:
Main Analysis

Data preparation
I follow the authors’ published Stata do-files (AngristLavy_Table2.do, AngristLavy_Table3.do) line-for-line: drop tiny and oversized classes (1 < classize < 45, c_size > 5), keep only schools that held the end-of-year exam (c_leom == 1), and drop schools with very high transient-student shares (c_pik < 3). avgverb and avgmath are scaled test scores; tipuach is Israel’s “percent disadvantaged” index (a composite of parental education, immigration, and family size); c_size is grade enrollment; classize is actual class size; and func1 is the Maimonides prediction \(f_{sc} = c_{sc} / (\text{int}((c_{sc}-1)/40) + 1)\).


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import statsmodels.api as sm
from statsmodels.formula.api import ols
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_stata("data/final4.dta")

# Scores are sometimes coded +100; undo the offset (matches the Stata .do file)
df.loc[df["avgverb"] > 100, "avgverb"] -= 100
df.loc[df["avgmath"] > 100, "avgmath"] -= 100

# Maimonides' rule prediction for class size
df["func1"] = df["c_size"] / (np.floor((df["c_size"] - 1) / 40) + 1)

# Blank out scores when the cell was empty
df.loc[df["verbsize"] == 0, ["avgverb", "passverb"]] = np.nan
df.loc[df["mathsize"] == 0, ["avgmath", "passmath"]] = np.nan

# Sample restrictions from the do-file
df = df[(df["classize"] > 1) & (df["classize"] < 45) & (df["c_size"] > 5)]
df = df[(df["c_leom"] == 1) & (df["c_pik"] < 3)]

print(f"Rows after filters: {len(df):,}")


Rows after filters: 2,053




Helper: cluster-robust OLS (by school)
Angrist and Lavy cluster their standard errors at the school level (and apply a Moulton correction on top; my SEs are cluster-robust but not Moulton-corrected, so they will be close but not identical to the published ones — the point estimates are what we are checking).


Code
def reg(formula, data, cluster="schlcode"):
    terms = [t.strip() for t in formula.split("~")[1].replace("+", " ").split()]
    lhs = formula.split("~")[0].strip()
    d = data.dropna(subset=[lhs] + terms + [cluster]).copy()
    m = ols(formula, data=d).fit(cov_type="cluster",
                                 cov_kwds={"groups": d[cluster]})
    return m, d

def summarize(m, keep):
    rows = []
    for v in keep:
        rows.append({"var": v,
                     "coef": round(m.params[v], 3),
                     "se": round(m.bse[v], 3)})
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)




Table II — naive OLS (the biased baseline)
This is the “wrong answer” the paper wants us to see first. We regress each test score on actual class size with no controls.


Code
t2c7, _  = reg("avgverb ~ classize", df)      # Col 7: reading, 4th grade
t2c10, _ = reg("avgmath ~ classize", df)      # Col 10: math, 4th grade

t2 = pd.DataFrame({
    "Col 7: Reading (avgverb)": [round(t2c7.params["classize"], 3),
                                 f'({round(t2c7.bse["classize"], 3)})',
                                 int(t2c7.nobs)],
    "Col 10: Math (avgmath)":   [round(t2c10.params["classize"], 3),
                                 f'({round(t2c10.bse["classize"], 3)})',
                                 int(t2c10.nobs)],
}, index=["classize coef", "(cluster SE)", "N"])
t2









Col 7: Reading (avgverb)
Col 10: Math (avgmath)




classize coef
0.141
0.221


(cluster SE)
(0.035)
(0.039)


N
2049
2049







The published Table II reports 0.141 (0.033) for column 7 and 0.221 (0.039) for column 10. The replication lands on the same point estimates, and the cluster SEs are within a hair of the Moulton-adjusted ones in the paper.
Read literally, each additional student in the class is associated with an extra 0.14 points on the reading test and 0.22 points on the math test — the opposite of what class-size advocates would expect. The punch line of the paper is that this sign reversal is not real; it is confounding. Tipuach-poor schools tend to have smaller classes, and tipuach is extremely predictive of achievement on its own.


Table III, Panel A — the reduced-form RDD
Now we use the Maimonides prediction func1 in place of actual class size, controlling for tipuach. Columns 7, 9, 11 correspond to the three outcomes (reading, math, actual class size) for the 4th-grade whole sample.


Code
d3 = df.dropna(subset=["avgverb"]).copy()      # paper restricts to non-missing verb

t3c7,  _ = reg("avgverb ~ func1 + tipuach", d3)   # Col 7:  reduced form, reading
t3c9,  _ = reg("avgmath ~ func1 + tipuach", d3)   # Col 9:  reduced form, math
t3c11, _ = reg("classize ~ func1 + tipuach", d3)  # Col 11: first stage, class size

t3 = pd.DataFrame({
    "Col 7: Reading":    [round(t3c7.params["func1"], 3),
                          f'({round(t3c7.bse["func1"], 3)})'],
    "Col 9: Math":       [round(t3c9.params["func1"], 3),
                          f'({round(t3c9.bse["func1"], 3)})'],
    "Col 11: Class size":[round(t3c11.params["func1"], 3),
                          f'({round(t3c11.bse["func1"], 3)})'],
}, index=["func1 coef", "(cluster SE)"])
t3









Col 7: Reading
Col 9: Math
Col 11: Class size




func1 coef
-0.085
0.038
0.772


(cluster SE)
(0.031)
(0.04)
(0.023)







The published Panel A reports −0.085 (0.034) for column 7, 0.038 (0.043) for column 9, and 0.772 (0.037) for column 11. Point estimates match.


Figure 1, Panel B — Maimonides’ rule vs. actual class size
The clearest way to see what Table III column 11 is capturing is to plot both the actual class size and the predicted class size against enrollment. The predicted series is a perfect sawtooth that collapses from ~40 to ~20 at every multiple of 40; the actual series roughly tracks the sawtooth but is smoothed by exceptions (integration of special-ed students, local staffing decisions, etc.). This is Figure 1 Panel B of the paper, for 4th grade.


Code
# Same 4th-grade analysis sample, averaged within each enrollment bin
g = (df.groupby("c_size")
       .agg(actual=("classize", "mean"),
            predicted=("func1", "mean"))
       .reset_index())
g = g[g["c_size"] <= 220]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5.2))
ax.plot(g["c_size"], g["predicted"], color="black", lw=1.3,
        label="Maimonides' rule (predicted)")
ax.plot(g["c_size"], g["actual"],    color="black", lw=1.0, ls=(0, (4, 3)),
        label="Actual class size")

# Cutoffs at 40, 80, 120, 160, 200
for c in [40, 80, 120, 160, 200]:
    ax.axvline(c, color="grey", lw=0.4, alpha=0.6)

ax.set_xlabel("Enrollment count")
ax.set_ylabel("Class size")
ax.set_title("Figure 1 Panel B — Class size by initial enrollment, 4th grade")
ax.set_xlim(0, 220)
ax.set_ylim(0, 45)
ax.legend(loc="lower right", frameon=False)
ax.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Figure 1, Panel B: Actual class size and Maimonides prediction by 4th-grade enrollment.




The solid sawtooth is the mechanical Maimonides prediction; it collapses from ~40 to ~20 at enrollments of 41, 81, 121, …. The dashed line is the actual average class size in the data: it sags more smoothly than the prediction because schools do not cleanly split a 41-kid grade into two classes of 20.5, but it clearly tracks the rule. That tracking is column 11 of Table III drawn as a picture — a one-unit move in func1 drags actual class size by ~0.77 units — and is what gives the RDD its identifying power.


What changed between the naive and reduced-form estimates
Three things are worth pulling out of Tables II and III together:

Sign flip for reading. Naive OLS says a one-student-bigger class raises reading by 0.14 points; the reduced form says a one-unit increase in the Maimonides prediction lowers reading by 0.09 points. Since func1 moves actual class size roughly 0.77-for-1 (column 11), the implied Wald / IV effect on reading is about \(-0.085 / 0.772 \approx -0.11\) — smaller classes help.
Math attenuates toward zero. Math goes from a strongly positive naive slope (+0.22) to a small and statistically insignificant reduced-form coefficient. Consistent with the paper, the class-size effect on 4th-grade math is noisier and does not survive the RDD correction.
Why the numbers moved. The naive OLS is fighting a confounder: schools that happen to have smaller classes tend to serve more disadvantaged students (higher tipuach) who score lower for reasons unrelated to class size. Once the Maimonides prediction is used in place of actual class size, the identifying variation comes from which side of 40, 80, 120 … a school’s enrollment falls on — variation that administrators did not choose.

title: Replication of Angrist & Lavy (1999)
section: Something Additional — why an RDD identifies a causal effect
text:
Something Additional — why an RDD identifies a causal effect
The empirical reversal between Table II and Table III deserves more than a shrug. Stepping back from the specific coefficients, here is the logic that makes a regression discontinuity design a credible path to causation, using Angrist and Lavy’s setup as the running example.
The cutoff. A “cutoff” is a hard rule that changes treatment assignment at a specific value of some underlying variable (the running variable). In this paper the running variable is 4th-grade enrollment and the cutoffs sit at multiples of 40: a school with 40 fourth-graders teaches them in one class of 40, but a school with 41 fourth-graders is required by Maimonides’ rule to split them into two classes that average 20.5. The “treatment” (class size) therefore falls off a cliff at 41, 81, 121, and so on — not because of what the school is like, but because of where its enrollment number sits relative to an arbitrary integer threshold.
Why schools near the cutoff look “as-if randomly assigned.” A school with 40 fourth-graders and a school with 41 fourth-graders are, for almost every purpose that matters to student achievement, indistinguishable: they draw from similar neighborhoods, hire teachers from the same labor market, and face the same curriculum. The one-child difference in enrollment is essentially random noise — families move, a child gets held back, a twin shows up. Yet that noise lands them on opposite sides of a rule that cuts average class size roughly in half. Formally, the RDD identifying assumption is that potential outcomes are continuous at the cutoff; because confounders are smooth in enrollment, the jump in outcomes at the threshold can only be caused by the jump in treatment. The paper supports this assumption by showing that tipuach and other covariates do not themselves jump at the cutoffs.
Why this avoids the selection bias that breaks the naive OLS. In the naive Table II regression, classize is the whole right-hand side, so the coefficient absorbs every reason a class ended up small, including deliberate policies that gave extra resources to struggling schools. That reverse channel (“low scores → smaller class”) biases the OLS slope upward, which is exactly what delivers the wrong-signed 0.14 and 0.22 in Table II. The reduced-form RDD in Table III breaks that channel by throwing away all the variation in classize that is not explained by the arbitrary rule, and keeping only the rule-induced variation. That surviving variation cannot plausibly be the consequence of test scores — Maimonides wrote the rule in the twelfth century, long before any Israeli fourth-grader took a standardized exam — so the coefficient on func1 is free of reverse causality, and, because enrollment itself is controlled for, free of the usual omitted-variable confounding too. What remains is a credible estimate of the causal effect of class size on achievement: smaller classes help reading, and the effect on math is close to zero in the 4th grade.
In short, RDD trades statistical power for credibility. It uses only the sliver of variation that a mechanical rule creates, and in return it gets to claim something OLS cannot: that the variation it is using was not caused by the outcome.

title: Replication of Angrist & Lavy (1999)
text:
References
Angrist, Joshua D., and Victor Lavy. 1999. “Using Maimonides’ Rule to Estimate the Effect of Class Size on Scholastic Achievement.” Quarterly Journal of Economics 114 (2): 533–575.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Imagine that I manage the homepage for a website that invites visitors to sign up for a newsletter. The sign-up box is small, but the words above it matter: a clearer or more persuasive call to action (CTA) could change how many visitors decide to subscribe.
For this experiment, I compare two possible CTAs:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!”

The goal is to learn which version leads to a higher sign-up rate. To do that, I use an A/B test. In an A/B test, visitors are randomly assigned to see one of two versions of a product, page, or message. Because assignment is random, the two groups should be similar except for the CTA they see. That lets us compare the sign-up rates and interpret the difference as evidence about which CTA performs better.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Imagine that I manage the homepage for a website that invites visitors to sign up for a newsletter. The sign-up box is small, but the words above it matter: a clearer or more persuasive call to action (CTA) could change how many visitors decide to subscribe.
For this experiment, I compare two possible CTAs:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!”

The goal is to learn which version leads to a higher sign-up rate. To do that, I use an A/B test. In an A/B test, visitors are randomly assigned to see one of two versions of a product, page, or message. Because assignment is random, the two groups should be similar except for the CTA they see. That lets us compare the sign-up rates and interpret the difference as evidence about which CTA performs better.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
Each visitor either signs up or does not sign up, so each outcome can be represented as a binary variable:
\[
X_i =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if visitor } i \text{ signs up}\\
0 & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]
A binary outcome like this is naturally modeled as a Bernoulli random variable. If a visitor sees CTA A, let the sign-up probability be \(\pi_A\). If a visitor sees CTA B, let the sign-up probability be \(\pi_B\).
The main quantity of interest is the difference in sign-up probabilities:
\[
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B.
\]
If \(\theta > 0\), CTA A performs better. If \(\theta < 0\), CTA B performs better. If \(\theta = 0\), the two CTAs perform equally well. In practice, we estimate this quantity with the difference in sample means:
\[
\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B.
\]
Because each observation is either 0 or 1, each sample mean is also a sample proportion.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In a real A/B test, the true sign-up probabilities would be unknown. For this exercise, however, I set the true values myself so that I can study whether the estimator behaves the way frequentist theory says it should.
I assume:
\[
\pi_A = 0.22, \qquad \pi_B = 0.18.
\]
So the true effect is:
\[
\theta = 0.22 - 0.18 = 0.04.
\]
That means CTA A is truly better in this simulated world, but only by 4 percentage points.

rng = np.random.default_rng(20260429)

n = 1000
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
theta_true = pi_A - pi_B

cta_a = rng.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
cta_b = rng.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

pi_hat_A = cta_a.mean()
pi_hat_B = cta_b.mean()
theta_hat = pi_hat_A - pi_hat_B

pd.DataFrame({
    "Group": ["CTA A", "CTA B", "Difference: A - B"],
    "True sign-up probability": [pi_A, pi_B, theta_true],
    "Observed sign-up rate": [pi_hat_A, pi_hat_B, theta_hat],
})








Group
True sign-up probability
Observed sign-up rate




0
CTA A
0.2200
0.2240


1
CTA B
0.1800
0.1780


2
Difference: A - B
0.0400
0.0460







In this simulated test, the estimated difference is close to the true difference of 4 percentage points, but it is not exactly equal to it because the sample is random.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers says that as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. In this setting, that gives us a reason to trust \(\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\): with enough visitors, the estimated difference in sample sign-up rates should get close to the true difference in sign-up probabilities.
To show this visually, I compute the element-wise difference between the CTA A and CTA B simulated draws. Each paired difference can be -1, 0, or 1. Then I plot the cumulative average of those differences as the sample size grows from 1 to 1,000.









Early in the plot, the running average moves around sharply because only a few observations have been included. As the number of observations grows, the curve becomes more stable and moves toward the true value of 0.04. This is the Law of Large Numbers in action: random noise is still present, but its influence becomes smaller as the sample grows.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
The point estimate tells us which CTA did better in the sample, but it does not tell us how precise that estimate is. A difference of 4 or 5 percentage points is more convincing when the standard error is small than when the standard error is large.
The bootstrap is a resampling method for estimating the variability of a statistic. The idea is to treat the observed sample as an approximation to the population. We repeatedly resample from the observed data with replacement, recompute the statistic each time, and use the spread of those bootstrap estimates as an estimate of uncertainty.

rng_boot = np.random.default_rng(20260430)
B = 1000

bootstrap_theta = np.empty(B)

for b in range(B):
    boot_a = rng_boot.choice(cta_a, size=n, replace=True)
    boot_b = rng_boot.choice(cta_b, size=n, replace=True)
    bootstrap_theta[b] = boot_a.mean() - boot_b.mean()

se_bootstrap = bootstrap_theta.std(ddof=1)

se_analytic = math.sqrt(
    pi_hat_A * (1 - pi_hat_A) / n +
    pi_hat_B * (1 - pi_hat_B) / n
)

ci_bootstrap = (
    theta_hat - 1.96 * se_bootstrap,
    theta_hat + 1.96 * se_bootstrap,
)

pd.DataFrame({
    "Quantity": [
        "Estimated effect",
        "Bootstrap standard error",
        "Analytical standard error",
        "95% CI lower bound",
        "95% CI upper bound",
    ],
    "Value": [
        theta_hat,
        se_bootstrap,
        se_analytic,
        ci_bootstrap[0],
        ci_bootstrap[1],
    ],
})








Quantity
Value




0
Estimated effect
0.0460


1
Bootstrap standard error
0.0178


2
Analytical standard error
0.0179


3
95% CI lower bound
0.0112


4
95% CI upper bound
0.0808







The bootstrap standard error and analytical standard error are very close, which is reassuring: the simulation-based estimate of uncertainty agrees with the formula-based estimate. The confidence interval is above zero in this simulated experiment, so the data provide evidence that CTA A performs better than CTA B.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem says that the sampling distribution of a sample mean becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows, even when the underlying data are not Normally distributed. That matters here because each visitor’s outcome is Bernoulli, not Normal. The CLT still tells us that the difference in sample means, \(\hat{\theta}\), should become approximately Normal when each group has enough observations.
To demonstrate this, I repeatedly simulate A/B tests at four sample sizes: 25, 50, 100, and 500 visitors per group. For each sample size, I repeat the experiment 1,000 times and plot the resulting values of \(\hat{\theta}\).









When the sample size is small, the histogram is relatively rough because there are fewer possible values for the difference in two proportions. As the sample size increases, the distribution becomes smoother and more bell-shaped. The distribution also gets narrower, which means the estimator becomes more precise with larger samples.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
The confidence interval suggests that CTA A may be better, but we can also frame the question as a formal hypothesis test. The null hypothesis is that the two CTAs have the same sign-up rate:
\[
H_0: \theta = 0.
\]
The alternative hypothesis is that the two CTAs have different sign-up rates:
\[
H_1: \theta \neq 0.
\]
To test this, we standardize the estimated effect:
\[
z = \frac{\hat{\theta} - 0}{SE(\hat{\theta})}.
\]
This standardization works because the CLT tells us that \(\hat{\theta}\) is approximately Normal for large samples. Under the null hypothesis, that distribution is centered at 0. Once we divide by the standard error, the test statistic is approximately standard Normal, which lets us calculate a p-value.
This test is sometimes casually called a “t-test” because it has the same basic structure as a two-sample t-test: estimate a difference, divide by a standard error, and compare the standardized statistic to a reference distribution. Strictly speaking, however, this Bernoulli A/B test is closer to a large-sample z-test. The exact classical t-test result assumes Normal data with an estimated variance. Here the data are binary, so the Normal approximation comes from the CLT.

z_stat = theta_hat / se_analytic
p_value = normal_two_sided_p(z_stat)

pd.DataFrame({
    "Quantity": ["Estimated effect", "Standard error", "z statistic", "p-value"],
    "Value": [theta_hat, se_analytic, z_stat, p_value],
})








Quantity
Value




0
Estimated effect
0.0460


1
Standard error
0.0179


2
z statistic
2.5709


3
p-value
0.0101







At the conventional \(\alpha = 0.05\) level, the p-value is small enough to reject the null hypothesis. In this simulated experiment, the data provide evidence that the two CTAs do not have the same sign-up rate, and the observed direction favors CTA A.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
The two-sample test can also be written as a simple regression. Stack the two groups into one dataset with:

\(Y_i\), equal to 1 if the visitor signed up and 0 otherwise
\(D_i\), equal to 1 if the visitor saw CTA A and 0 if the visitor saw CTA B

Then estimate:
\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i.
\]
The intercept, \(\beta_0\), is the expected sign-up rate when \(D_i = 0\), which is the mean of the CTA B group. The fitted value for CTA A is \(\beta_0 + \beta_1\). Therefore, \(\beta_1\) is the difference between the CTA A and CTA B means:
\[
\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta.
\]

y = np.concatenate([cta_b, cta_a])
d = np.concatenate([np.zeros(n), np.ones(n)])
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(2 * n), d])

beta = np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X) @ (X.T @ y)
residuals = y - X @ beta
sigma2 = (residuals @ residuals) / (len(y) - X.shape[1])
cov_beta = sigma2 * np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X)

reg_beta = beta[1]
reg_se = math.sqrt(cov_beta[1, 1])
reg_t = reg_beta / reg_se
reg_p = normal_two_sided_p(reg_t)

pd.DataFrame({
    "Quantity": [
        "Coefficient on CTA A indicator",
        "Regression standard error",
        "t statistic",
        "p-value",
    ],
    "Value": [reg_beta, reg_se, reg_t, reg_p],
})








Quantity
Value




0
Coefficient on CTA A indicator
0.0460


1
Regression standard error
0.0179


2
t statistic
2.5696


3
p-value
0.0102







The regression coefficient on the CTA A indicator exactly matches the difference in sample means from the earlier calculation. The standard error and p-value are also very close to the two-sample results. Any small difference comes from how the variance is estimated: the simple OLS regression uses a pooled residual variance, while the earlier two-sample formula allowed the two Bernoulli variances to differ by group.
This equivalence matters because regression is flexible. The same framework can handle more complicated experiments with controls, interactions, fixed effects, or more than two treatment arms. In the simple A/B test, regression reproduces the familiar difference-in-means test; in more realistic settings, it extends the same logic.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
Suppose my boss does not want to wait until the experiment is complete. Instead, she wants to check the results after every 100 visitors per group: after 100, 200, 300, and so on up to 1,000. If any test is significant at the 5% level, she wants to stop early and declare a winner.
That sounds efficient, but it creates a serious problem under classical frequentist testing. A single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% false positive rate when the null hypothesis is true. But if we repeatedly test accumulating data, each peek is another chance to get a false positive. The probability of at least one false rejection across all peeks can be much larger than 5%.
To show this, I simulate a world where the null hypothesis is true:
\[
\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20.
\]
In that world, neither CTA is actually better. I then simulate 10,000 experiments. In each experiment, I peek after every 100 observations per group and record whether any p-value falls below 0.05.

rng_peek = np.random.default_rng(20260431)
peeking_reps = 10000
peek_points = np.arange(100, 1001, 100)
critical_value = 1.959963984540054

hits = 0
batch_size = 1000

for start in range(0, peeking_reps, batch_size):
    current_batch = min(batch_size, peeking_reps - start)

    null_a = rng_peek.binomial(1, 0.20, size=(current_batch, n))
    null_b = rng_peek.binomial(1, 0.20, size=(current_batch, n))

    cum_a = np.cumsum(null_a, axis=1)[:, peek_points - 1]
    cum_b = np.cumsum(null_b, axis=1)[:, peek_points - 1]

    p_a_now = cum_a / peek_points
    p_b_now = cum_b / peek_points

    se_now = np.sqrt(
        p_a_now * (1 - p_a_now) / peek_points +
        p_b_now * (1 - p_b_now) / peek_points
    )

    z_now = np.divide(
        p_a_now - p_b_now,
        se_now,
        out=np.zeros_like(se_now),
        where=se_now > 0,
    )

    hits += np.any(np.abs(z_now) > critical_value, axis=1).sum()

peeking_false_positive_rate = hits / peeking_reps

pd.DataFrame({
    "Testing strategy": ["One planned test", "Ten repeated peeks"],
    "False positive rate": [0.05, peeking_false_positive_rate],
})








Testing strategy
False positive rate




0
One planned test
0.0500


1
Ten repeated peeks
0.2009
















The simulated false positive rate with repeated peeking is much higher than the nominal 5% rate. This happens even though the null hypothesis is true and there is no real difference between the CTAs.
The practical lesson is that A/B tests need a stopping rule. If we plan to test once at the end, we should wait until the planned sample size is reached. If we want to monitor results during the experiment, we should use a method designed for sequential testing rather than repeatedly applying an ordinary 5% test.
